food_subgroup: Toasted wheat vermicelli in milk and coconut milk with sugar and ghee-fried nuts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Semiya Payasam. Nearest parent is a vermicelli milk dessert. A new concept for South Indian payasam preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vermicelli in coconut milk/milk dessert with sugar, South Indian (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Semiya Payasam; A14AS (pasta/vermicelli-based dessert) used as closest parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Semiya Payasam is approximately 150 g (one small bowl), as commonly served at South Indian restaurants and festive occasions.

nutrient_source.title: Payasam (Semiya) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry EX000079
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for Semiya Payasam (EX000079). Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are supplemented from enriched JSON with confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wheat Vermicelli and Milk-Based Desserts — University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate: wheat vermicelli in full-fat coconut milk and cow's milk with sugar)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Semiya Payasam. GI of 62 estimated from closest analogous foods in the University of Sydney GI Database: wheat vermicelli (GI ~47–55 plain) adjusted upward for added sugar and coconut milk fat moderating effect. Jaggery has a slightly lower GI than white sugar; coconut milk fat moderates the glycaemic spike. Overall carbohydrate load per 150 g serving yields GL ~26.5. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~55 mg/100 g; ~83 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Semiya Payasam is naturally very low in sodium and poses minimal concern for blood pressure management; individuals with hypertension may consume a standard serving without sodium-related risk, though overall caloric and sugar intake should still be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~62) combined with high sugar content (16.5 g/100 g) and significant glycaemic load (~26.5 per 150 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Semiya Payasam to half a serving (approximately 75 g) or choose a reduced-sugar or jaggery-substituted version, as the combined glycaemic load of wheat vermicelli and added sugar creates a substantial postprandial glucose response despite the partial moderating effect of coconut milk fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (5.5 g/100 g; ~8.25 g per serving) primarily from coconut milk and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from coconut milk and ghee (predominantly medium-chain triglycerides) contributes approximately 8.25 g per standard serving, which approaches the upper daily limit for saturated fat in cardiovascular disease management; individuals with CVD should limit portion size to 75–100 g and consume Semiya Payasam only occasionally.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Semiya Payasam is a classic South Indian festival dessert made from thin wheat vermicelli (semiya) toasted in ghee until golden, then simmered in a mixture of full-fat cow's milk and coconut milk, sweetened with sugar or jaggery, and flavoured with green cardamom and saffron. The dish is finished with ghee-fried cashews and raisins, yielding a creamy, fragrant, slightly thick dessert soup served warm or at room temperature. It is a staple of the Onam Sadya feast in Kerala, a Deepavali sweet in Tamil Nadu, and a festive offering in South Indian temples as prasadam. Nutritionally, a 150 g serving provides approximately 293 kcal, 6.3 g protein, 12.75 g fat (8.25 g saturated), and 42.75 g carbohydrate with 24.75 g sugar. The moderate glycaemic index (~62) and high glycaemic load (~26.5) make it a food to be consumed in moderation by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat from coconut milk and ghee warrants portion control for those managing cardiovascular disease. Sodium is very low (~83 mg per serving), making it suitable for hypertension from a sodium perspective. Semiya Payasam differs from North Indian kheer in its use of coconut milk and wheat vermicelli rather than pure cow's milk and rice.
common_user_queries: how many calories in a bowl of payasam

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (EX000079), the primary approved source for Indian dishes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous foods with documented basis. Minor trace nutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched JSON at confidence 0.9.
notes: Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace per ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. Portions deduplicated from input (three identical 150 g entries collapsed to one canonical serving). The food_subgroup description clarifies the use of both cow's milk and coconut milk as is typical for Semiya Payasam. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Semiya Payasam is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values carry confidence 0.9 from enriched JSON source.
